coredata.dc:description: Surface engineering of carbon nanodots (C-dots) is a key bridge to connect the C-dots with biomedical applications. In this chapter, recent developments in functionalizing carbon nanodots toward bioimaging and biosensing applications will be reviewed with special focus on their applications in living cells, tissues, and animals. In particular, our work on the synthesis and surface engineering of C-dots for intracellular Zn2+ detection and multimodal bioimaging in vivo will be highlighted. This chapter will be concluded by outlining potential directions in engineering C-dots for multiplexed detection, multimodal imaging, and theranostics.

Schematic illustration of C-dot preparation via top-down and bottom-up approach [9–11].
Top-down approach (A) [15] and bottom-up approach (B) [16] for the preparation of N-doped C-dots.

(B) The UV-Vis absorption spectrum (curve a), excitation spectrum (curve b), and emission spectra (curve c) of GCDs. The inset shows the photograph of GCDs under daylight and 365nm UV light. (C) T 1-weighted MR images of GCDs with various concentrations and a linear correlation between the longitudinal relaxivity (r 1) and equivalent Gd concentration of GCDs [40].
Synthesis routes for the quinoline-derivative-functionalized C-dots (A) [50] and the DTPA-functionalized C-dots (B) [51] via carbodiimide chemistry.

Illustration of C-dot-based fluorescence turn-on nanoprobe for targeted imaging of FR-overexpressed cancer cells (A) [56] and discriminative detection of GSH over Cys/Hcy with both colorimetric and fluorometric readout (B) [57].

Schematic illustration of C-dot-based nanosensor for Zn2+, K+, and Pb2+ sensing: (A) surface functionalization of C-dots with specific receptors [50], (B) development of a FRET system using C-dots as donor [93], and (C) development of an ECL sensor using C-dots as luminophores [94].

Schematic representation of simultaneously sensing BRAC1 and TK1 using fluorescent C-dots and hairpin structure as fluorescence reporters [144].

chapter.ce:label: Chapter 5
chapter.ce:title: Surface Engineering of Carbon Nanodots (C-Dots) for Biomedical Applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Sensing Technology and Biomedical Instruments (Guangdong Province) School of Biomedical Engineering, Sun Yat-Sen University, Guangzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Sensing Technology and Biomedical Instruments (Guangdong Province) School of Biomedical Engineering, Sun Yat-Sen University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Surface engineering of carbon nanodots (C-dots) is a key bridge to connect the C-dots with biomedical applications. In this chapter, recent developments in functionalizing carbon nanodots toward bioimaging and biosensing applications will be reviewed with special focus on their applications in living cells, tissues, and animals. In particular, our work on the synthesis and surface engineering of C-dots for intracellular Zn2+ detection and multimodal bioimaging in vivo will be highlighted. This chapter will be concluded by outlining potential directions in engineering C-dots for multiplexed detection, multimodal imaging, and theranostics.

ce:section.ce:para: In this section, we briefly introduce C-dots first and then summarize their synthesis strategies in a concise way, with focus on their preparation via both top-down and bottom-up routes.

Since their discovery in 2004 by Xu et al. when using gel electrophoresis to purify arc-synthesized single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWNTs) [1], carbon nanodots (C-dots) have attracted worldwide research attentions. Especially after the fluorescent C-dots are synthesized in 2006 via laser ablation of a carbon target by Sun and coworkers [2], dramatically, growing research attentions have been drawn to understand their photoluminescence (PL) mechanism, design the new synthetic routes, engineer their surface properties and functionalities, and explore their applications.

C-dot is believed to be composed of a crystalline or amorphous core (sp2 carbons and/or sp3 carbons) and carbonaceous surface. The reported C-dots exhibit two different morphologies: spherical or sheet. The spherical C-dots are considered as spherical nanoparticles (NPs) with diameter less than 10nm, while the sheet form of C-dots is regarded as layered sheets possessing lateral size less than 100nm. Despite the quite different morphologies, we merged them into C-dots in this chapter, because they usually exhibit the same luminescence properties due to their similar sp2 carbon structures and surface functional groups, and the multilayered nanosheets are close to quasi-spherical structure [3].

Although the exact mechanism is still an open question, C-dot light emission can be attributed to quantum confinement effect, surface state, and molecule state [4]. Similar to semiconductor quantum dots, the quantum confinement effect of C-dots could be simply expressed as their light emission is closely related to their sizes. A typical example given here is a series of C-dots with diameter ranging from 1.2 to 3.8nm prepared by electrochemical method. The fluorescence emission exhibited significant redshift as their size increases. The theoretical calculations and the corresponding HOMO-LUMO gaps for C-dots in different sizes validate that the light emission of C-dots originates from the quantum-sized graphite structure [5].

Another well-established theory is that finite size, surface functionalities, and element doping can induce emissive traps and structural defects onto C-dots, thus resulting in their different emission colors. A well-recognized example is presented by Xiong and coworkers, where a set of full-color light-emitting C-dots are synthesized by tuning its surface oxidation degree (Fig. 5.1 A) [6].

Molecule-state mechanism claims that the PL centers of C-dots consist of the organic fluorophores that can directly emit fluorescence and present on the surface or interior of carbon backbone. Giannelis and coworkers verify this hypothesis by synthesizing C-dots with both molecule and carbon core states ( Fig. 5.1B). Their results demonstrated that amide-containing fluorophores are generated on C-dots after precursors are treated with pyrolysis. And this hypothesis is further validated by the reduced quantum yield of C-dots when higher temperature pyrolysis is applied, because the molecular fluorophores are expected to be more easily consumed at higher temperature [7].

Since its discovery, a considerable variety of methods have been designed and well developed for C-dot synthesis, and these methods can be categorized into two different approaches, namely, top-down approach and bottom-up approach [8]. The main features of C-dot preparation are the easily obtainable and low-cost precursors, such as small molecules, amino acids, polymers, or some natural sources, as well as efficient and abundant synthesis methods, including laser ablation, hydrothermal carbonization, pyrolysis, combustion or thermal microwave heating, and chemical or electrochemical oxidation.

In top-down approach, the C-dots are obtained by breaking down or cleaving the block carbon-based materials into smaller ones through chemical or physical methods (Fig. 5.2 ). For example, the first reported C-dots are synthesized by laser ablation of graphite in the presence of water vapor with argon as a carrier gas under 900°C and 75kPa, followed by surface passivation and/or acid oxidative treatment [2]. Afterward, through reducing the size of graphene, graphite, carbon nanotubes, activated carbon, carbon soot, nanodiamonds, or other materials that own an sp2 carbon structure but have no efficient bandgap to emit fluorescence, a large number of top-down methods have been developed, such as arc discharge, electrochemical or acid oxidation, nanolithography by reactive-ion etching (RIE), hydrothermal or solvothermal reduction, sonication or microwave-assisted chemical exfoliation, and photo-Fenton reaction [12]. However, some of these methods are relatively complicated and in need of large equipments and hazardous conditions, which restricts the large-scale production of C-dots. Besides, surface passivation or functionalization of C-dots is always required for a postsynthetic treatment in order to achieve a higher quantum yield, which accumulates more steps to the synthesis.

In bottom-up approach, the C-dots are formed by assembling small organic molecules under a range of different reaction conditions (Fig. 5.2). There are various successful examples for this approach such as hydrothermal treatment of citric acid or carbohydrates and stepwise chemical fusion using benzene derivatives, carbonization, and pyrolysis of precursors. Specifically, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine groups in the carbon precursors, such as ascorbic acid, glycerol, and amino acid, can make them easier to dehydrate and carbonize under elevated temperatures [12]. For example, Chi et al. prepared a kind of polyamine-functionalized C-dots in aqueous solution through a facile pyrolysis treatment of citric acid and branched polyethylenimine (BPEI). Benefit from the surface capping of BPEI, the as-prepared C-dots exhibit a high quantum yield and sensitivity toward Cu2+ in aqueous solution [13]. The bottom-up approach can improve the controllability of the size, shape, and surface properties of C-dots during their synthesis by selecting the designed precursors. In addition, these methods are usually low cost and quite efficient for preparing fluorescent C-dots on a large scale, which is necessary for the practical applications of novel materials.

Recently, intense research attention has been emphasized on improving C-dot functionality. In this regard, two main strategies to control the optical, chemical, and electric properties of C-dots are element doping during their synthesis and surface functionalization after their synthesis. For doping strategy, atomic impurities are introduced into C-dots to tune their electronic structure. For surface functionalization strategy, functional molecules or polymers are conjugated onto the surface of C-dots via covalent or noncovalent bond. In this section, recent progress in improving C-dot functionality via doping and surface functionalization strategies has been summarized and discussed. In addition, insight on how to control C-dot properties is also offered.

Although the pristine C-dots already have their original features for numerous applications, they are still restricted by many inherent drawbacks. For example, most as-prepared C-dots have relatively low emission efficiency compared with semiconductor quantum dots, even after surface passivation, so the limitation in their quantum yield largely weakens their performance in sensing and imaging applications. In order to maximize their PL capabilities and biomedical potential, many research groups have made significant efforts. Up to now, there are two popular ways for C-dot remolding, namely, atomic doping and surface modification. Doping means to incorporate atomic impurities into the carbon cores to change their electronic structures and create n-type and p-type carriers. As a result, some features dominating by HOMO-LUMO energy gaps, such as electronic and optical properties, could be fine-tuned. Furthermore, doping with specific rare elements, such as gadolinium or terbium, some special functions could be endowed to C-dots [14]. In regard of surface functionalization, various strategies are developed to change the surface states of C-dots by tuning its surface functional groups and to endow C-dots with more functionalities by conjugating specific molecules or polymers. These two approaches are the most promising avenues to extend the possibility and versatility of C-dots in all regions of applications [8]. Actually, the C-dots with different optical and surface properties can be synthesized using different precursors and methods (Table 5.1 ).

Chemical doping is served as a convenient and general strategy to tune the optical and electron-transfer properties of C-dots (Table 5.1). Facile bottom-up approach rather than top-down approach, such as microwave, ultrasonication, and hydrothermal synthesis, is more applicable to introduce dopants into C-dots. Usually, organic molecular precursors containing the doping elements are directly added to the reaction mixture to produce the doped C-dots. The doping elements chosen can be generally classified into heteroatoms (nonmetal) and metal atoms. Besides, in terms of the number of the dopants, there are two types of doping, namely, single doping and multidoping.

Since nitrogen possesses a similar atomic size to carbon atoms, it has become the most adopted dopant atoms in C-dots. And the nitrogen atom could inject electrons into C-dots and change the internal electronic structure after successful doping, which effectively improves its emission property, thus facilitating its applications in both imaging and sensing. Various synthetic routes have been successfully developed to incorporate nitrogen into the carbon framework for the preparation of highly fluorescent N-doped C-dots.

Leveraging the abundance and sustainability of natural biomass materials, top-down approaches could provide a cheap and green route for C-dot production (Fig. 5.3 A). Sun and coworkers report the preparation of N-doped C-dots through the hydrothermal treatment of grass at 180°C for 3h [17]. The obtained C-dots are composed of 41.54% C, 4.18% H, 50.05% O, and 4.23% N and emit blue fluorescence with 6.2% QY. Following the similar protocol, C-dots with around 10% N-content have also been prepared successfully by hydrothermal treatment of soy milk and Bombyx mori silk [15, 18]. However, restricted by the uncontrollable amount of doped nitrogen, to obtain satisfactory PL quantum yield (QY) is still challenging.

Since one of the advantages of the bottom-up approach is its good controllability of the size, shape, and surface properties of C-dots, this enables the bottom-up approach a more appealing choice to introduce dopants into C-dots (Fig. 5.3B). Bottom-up synthetic routes rely on choosing appropriate small molecular precursors containing higher nitrogen contents and suitable treatment methods such as microwave, ultrasonic-assisted, solvothermal, and chemical oxidation. For example, color-tunable N-doped C-dots are synthesized by solvothermal treatment of aminosalicylic acid. A QY of 16.4% is obtained as the result of improvement effect induced by doped nitrogen atoms [19]. C-dots with a QY up to 75% are synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and tris(hydroxymethyl)methyl aminomethane. And the resultant C-dots exhibit excellent thermal stability and photostability, probably due to the chemical composition and steric effect of the nitrogen doping in C-dots [16]. Similarly, yellowish-green fluorescence emitting C-dots with 73.2% QY are also prepared by a simple and solvent-free synthetic strategy using citric acid and dicyandiamide as precursors [20]. Although the exact mechanism is still a controversial issue, it is believed that the nitrogen doping can introduce a new kind of surface state by which the trapped electrons are able to facilitate a high yield of radiative recombination [12].

Stronger electron donors than nitrogen, such as sulfur, have also been doped into C-dots to tune their optical properties [21–24]. Especially, the sulfur atom in C-dots plays a role as catalyst for oxidation-reduction reaction, which introduces more passivated surface defects to C-dots and further enhances their PL. For example, S-doped C-dots with QY of 11.8% can be prepared by the pyrolysis of thiomalic acid (TMA) in the presence of sulfuric acid at 90°C for 4h [21]. Through optimizing its synthesis routes, S-doped C-dots exhibiting higher QY of 67% can also be prepared by hydrothermal treatment of sodium citrate and sodium thiosulfate [23]. Bandosz and coworkers demonstrated that the specific configuration of sulfur determined the PL capability of the S-doped C-dots, since sulfones, sulfoxides, and sulfonic acids are acting as chromophores [24].

Other dopants such as phosphorus (P) and boron (B) are also appealing choices for tuning C-dot optical properties. Phosphorus is an n-type donor to form substitutional defects in diamond sp3 thin films, so doping P atoms into C-dots could modulate their optical and electronic properties. As anticipated, doping phosphorous atoms into C-dots through solvent-thermal treatment of hydroquinone and phosphorus tribromide not only tunes C-dot emission band but also significantly improves their QY to 25% [25]. Similarly, P-doped C-dots are facilely prepared by hydrothermal treatment of a sucrose phosphate solution and further conditioned for the detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) via a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process [26]. Unexpectedly, the phenomenon of aggregation-induced redshift emission is observed for P-doped C-dots that are prepared by hydrothermal treatment of triethyl phosphonoacetate in the presence of phosphoric acid [27]. The possible reason is that the doping with the electron-rich phosphorus might result in an electron-rich system in C-dots. Boron is also capable of serving as an active site for charge transfer, since it is the left neighbor of carbon in the periodic table of elements. As expected, the as-prepared C-dots containing 5.0% B in weight exhibited bright blue fluorescence with a QY of up to 14.8% [28]. A recent investigation conducted by Nanda and coworkers reveals that the QY of B-doped C-dots increased with the increase of B content, and its size and B content could be tuned by controlling the hydrothermal reaction time [29].

It is reasonable to deduce that the synergistic effect between the different doped atoms might create unique electronic structures and/or introduce more active sites, thus tuning the optical properties of C-dots and broadening its applications [30–34]. Therefore, codoping different atoms into C-dots has attracted increasingly research attentions. For example, due to the synergistic effect between the doped N and S atoms, N-S codoped C-dots that are prepared by a one-step hydrothermal treatment of l-cysteine and CA exhibit excitation-independent emission with very high QY (73%) [30]. More importantly, Yu and coworkers make an attempt to explain how the doped atoms affect the optical properties of C-dots. In brief, C-dots without any dopant (O-C-dots) have different kinds of surface states (labeled as O-states) and thus exhibit excitation-dependent emission spectra and a broad UV/Vis absorption band. After doped with N atoms, a new kind of surface state (labeled as the N-state) that is advantageous to highly effective radiative recombination is formed, and the resultant N-C-dots therefore exhibit a significantly higher QY. But N-C-dots still exhibit broad and excitation-dependent emission spectra, because the density of the N-state is comparable with that of those O-states in the N-C-dots. However, S atoms are capable of eliminating the O-states and enhancing the N-state, resulting in an even higher QY and excitation-independent emission of N-S-C-dots [30].

By carbonization of adenosine-5′-triphosphate, P-N-codoped C-dots emitting strong blue fluorescence with a QY of 23.5% are successfully prepared [31]. Through microwave-assisted pyrolysis of the mixtures containing aniline, ethylene diamine, and phosphoric acid for 40s, P-N-codoped C-dots emitting green and blue fluorescence under different excitation wavelengths are synthesized, and the resultant C-dots exhibit a QY up to 51% [32]. Using a hydrothermal approach with N-(4-hydroxyphenyl)glycine and boric acid as precursors, highly fluorescent B-N-codoped C-dots emitting strong green fluorescence with a QY of 11.44% are also successfully prepared [33]. Following a similar hydrothermal approach using 2,5-diaminobenzene sulfonic acid and 4-aminophenylboronic acid hydrochloride at 1:1 weight ratio, B-N-S-codoped C-dots exhibiting red emission with a QY of 5.44% are successfully synthesized and conditioned for Fe3+ quantitation with a detection limit of 90nM [34].

Metal atoms such as Zn [35], Tb [36], Gd [37–41], Cu [42], Ge [43], Co [44], and Mg [45] are also exploited for doping C-dots to improve its optical properties and integrate new functionalities. For example, Zn-doped C-dots emitting strong blue fluorescence with a QY of 32.3% have been prepared by a one-step hydrothermal treatment of sodium citrate and zinc chloride [35]. By simple carbonization of the mixture of CA and terbium(III) nitrate pentahydrate at 190°C for 30min, Tb atoms can also be successfully doped into C-dots [36].

Gd3+ is able to curtail the longitudinal relaxation time of hydrogen protons, thus providing MR signal enhancement. Therefore, doping Gd might render C-dots the magnetic resonance (MR) capability and obtain the FL/MR dual-imaging probe to combine high sensitivity of FL imaging and deep tissue penetration of MR imaging. The Gd-doped C-dots (GCDs) can be synthesized through one-pot calcination of gadopentetic acid (Gd-DTPA) [37]; gadopentetate monomeglumine (GdPM) [38]; or a mixture of gadopentetic acid, betaine hydrochloride, and tris base [39]. However, the poor hydrophilicity and relatively larger sizes of the as-prepared GCDs result in nonnegligible accumulation in the reticuloendothelial organs, thus limiting their in vivo applications. Therefore, hydrothermal approaches with and without microwave assistance under mild condition have been demonstrated for the successful synthesis of GCDs that exhibit high colloidal stability and water solubility under physiological conditions [40, 41]. In a recent work of our research group, Gd-doped C-dots were synthesized by one-pot pyrolysis of CA, Gd-DTPA, and branched polyethylenimine (BPEI) (Fig. 5.4 A). The obtained GCDs exhibit excitation-dependent emission spectra and a strong fluorescent brightness with absolute QY of 40% (Fig. 5.4B) and a high MR response with longitudinal relaxation of 57.42mM−1 s−1 (Fig. 5.4C). Its dual-modality bioimaging is demonstrated successfully using mice as models, showing its great promise as high-performance functional imaging probes [40].

In addition, codoping of metal atoms such as Cu [42], Mg [43], and Co [44] and nonmetal dopants such as N is also reported to tune the emission properties of C-dots. For example, Zeng et al. successfully synthesize Cu-N-doped C-dots through a one-step pyrolysis approach using Na2[Cu(EDTA)] as precursor. Experimental results confirm that the codoping of Cu-N into C-dots results in 2.5 and 1.5 times enhancement in their electron-accepting and electron-donating abilities and their significantly improved photocatalytic efficiency (3.5-fold) in the photooxidation of 1,4-DHP in aqueous solution [42]. Codoping of Mg and N has been achieved by hydrothermal treatment of CA and Mg(OH)2 at 200°C for 3h. It is believed that the Mg-CA chelate can introduce Mg into carbon matrix and preserve carboxylic groups for subsequent N passivation, thus contributing to a significantly improved QY (83.0% using quinine sulfate as a standard) [43]. Co- and N-codoped C-dots with controllable concentrations of Co and N can be prepared through the pyrolysis of carbon quantum dots and vitamin B12 by changing their initial mass ratios [44].

Accumulating publications confirm that doping is a highly efficient way to improve the emission properties of C-dots possibly through modulating its band structure or passivating the surface active sites by the stabilization of the excitons and even to integrate new functionalities such as new imaging modality and photothermal therapy. Though the relation between the emission properties of doped C-dots and its dopant content is still an open question, more and more evidences indicate that a higher dopant content in C-dots leads to a higher QY. The exact mechanism of tuning C-dot properties by doping will continue to be the research focus, which can facilitate designing and conditioning of doped C-dots for fascinating biomedical applications such as biosensing, bioimaging, and theranostics.

Surface functionalization with specific inorganic, organic, biological, or polymeric materials is both an effective pathway to tune the emission properties of C-dots and a key bridge to connect the C-dots with fascinating biomedical applications [45]. Generally, C-dots are regarded as core-shell structure containing a carbon core and surface passivation with various functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine. The surface groups can provide not only the surface defects for FL generation but also the reactive sites for specific functionalization.

Through tailoring the synthesis precursors, C-dots with various functional groups such as carboxyls, amines, alkoxysilanes, and quaternary ammoniums can be prepared [8, 14]. Due to the well-established and easy-to-accomplish conjugation protocols, amines and carboxyls on C-dots are the most popular reactive sites for further surface functionalization. For the preparation of fluorescent C-dots with surface carboxyls, CA and polyethylene glycol (PEG) are the popular precursors, due to their abundant carboxyls that can also facilitate the dehydration and carbonization process [46, 47]. For example, Lu et al. first synthesize the carboxyl-capped C-dots by direct pyrolysis of CA at 180°C under air atmosphere [46]. C-dots with abundant surface carboxyls can also be prepared by a hydrothermal approach using PEG with different molar weights (400–6000gmol−1) as the sole precursor [47]. Although C-dots derived from PEG with larger molecular weight give a slightly larger particle size and a relatively wider size distribution, the obtained products exhibit almost identical morphology.

Same to N atom doping, in order to synthesize C-dots with surface amine groups, amine-abundant small molecules such as ethylenediamine (EDA), tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA), 2,2′-(ethylene-dioxy) bis(ethylamine) (EDBEA), and urea or polymers like chitosan, branched polyethylenimine (BPEI), and PEG diamine (PEG1500N) are the extensively employed N resource, and microwave irradiation, hydrothermal carbonization, and pyrolysis are the popular synthesis strategies. A typical example is the low-temperature carbonization of CA in the presence of BPEI to prepare amino-terminated C-dots [48]. By surface functionalization of C-dots with EDA via a hydrothermal approach, amino-terminated C-dots have also been successfully prepared [49].

Surface functionalization is a multistep process. After obtaining the desired surface functional groups, a proper conjugating strategy should be designed to anchor specific functionalities onto C-dots to address specific requirements. Generally, three conjugation strategies have been well documented for surface functionalization of C-dots, namely, covalent bonds, hydrogen bonds, and electrostatic interactions.

Covalent bonds are more robust than any other intermolecular forces. Because of the abundant amino and carboxyl groups in biomolecules, carbodiimide chemistry involving a classical catalyst couple, ethyl(dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) is the most popular approach for surface engineering of C-dots via covalent bonds. In our previous works, quinoline derivative and cyclic DTPA dianhydride (cDTPAA) that respectively serve as the specific zinc probe and the specific Gd3+ chelation ligands have been successfully conjugated onto the surface of amino-terminated C-dots via carbodiimide chemistry under the catalysis of EDC/NHS (Fig. 5.5 ) [50, 51]. The quinoline derivative-functionalized C-dots can act as a zinc-specific receptor, since zinc ion can trigger the fluorescence color change from blue to green excitation of 350nm wavelength (Fig. 5.5A) [50]. While after subsequently chelated with Gd3+ to form Gd-DTPA-functionalized C-dots, the C-dots can also be successfully conditioned as an FL/MR dual-imaging probe (Fig. 5.5B) [51].

Besides the covalent bond formation between amino and carboxyl groups, amino groups can also be easily conjugated with isothiocyanate, sulfonyl chloride, and carbon disulfide groups. Typical examples are illustrated in Fig. 5.6 . In Diac's study, rhodamine B (RhB) isothiocyanate is conjugated onto C-dot surface through the reaction between amino and isothiocyanate groups to develop FRET-based sensing platforms and photovoltaic devices (Fig. 5.6A) [52]. Taking advantages of the reaction between amino and carbon disulfide groups, bis(dithiocarbamato)copper (II) can be functionalized onto C-dot surface, and the resultant C-dots exhibit specific fluorescence “turn-on” response toward Hg2+ (Fig. 5.6B) [53]. Ye and coworkers covalently conjugated a europium complex (BHHCT-Eu3+) onto C-dot surface basing on the reaction between amino and sulfonyl chloride groups to develop a nanoprobe with dual emissions for the ratiometric detection of Cu2+ in aqueous media (Fig. 5.6C) [54]. In addition, surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP) has also been successfully demonstrated for surface engineering C-dots for developing a PDMA-functionalized C-dot-based platform for serum-resistant gene delivery and bioimaging. In this approach, amino-terminated C-dots are converted to Br-terminated C-dots by reacting surface amino groups with 2-bromoisobutyric acid with the assistance of N,N′-carbonyldiimidazole activation. Then, Br-terminated C-dots are mixed with 2-(dimethylamino) ethyl methacrylate (DMAEMA), N,N,N′,N″,N″- pentamethyldiethylenetriamine (PMDETA), and NaCl in the degassed ethanol suspension of CuCl and allowed to react for 3h at room temperature. Subsequently, MPDSAH is injected into the mixture and allowed to react for another 24h at room temperature to prepare PDMA-functionalized C-dots (Fig. 5.6D) [55].

Although possessing a weaker interaction force than the covalent bonds, hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions are also proper ways for surface engineering C-dots. Actually, due to their inherent dynamic features, noncovalent bonds can facilitate the design of competitive reaction-based sensing assays. For example, because of the surface-abundant –OH, –COOH on C-dots and sufficient –NH2, –OH, and –COOH groups in folic acid (FA), strong hydrogen-bond interaction between FA and C-dots is anticipated. Taking advantages of the hydrogen-bond interaction between FA and C-dots, a fluorescence turn-on nanoprobe is prepared and applied for targeted imaging of cancer cells [56]. As illustrated in Fig. 5.7 A, FA can quench the fluorescence of C-dots via a FRET process. However, when folate receptor (FR) is presented, FA will preferentially react with FR due to the stronger affinity of FR-FA complex than the hydrogen bond between FA and C-dots, leading to the change of FRET efficiency and thus turning on C-dot fluorescence.

Taking advantages of electrostatic interactions between positively charged C-dots and negatively charged gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), a dual-mode nanosensor is developed for glutathione (GSH) discrimination over Cys/Hcy basing on two distance-dependent phenomenons: color change of AuNPs and FRET [57]. As illustrated in Fig. 5.7B, electrostatic interaction between amino-terminated C-dots and carboxyl-terminated AuNPs induces the aggression of NPs, resulting in color of solution changing from red to blue and turning off C-dot fluorescence. However, due to the multidentate anchor of GSH toward AuNPs and stronger affinity than electrostatic interactions between C-dots and AuNPs, GSH will encapsulate AuNPs in priority, thus changing solution color from blue to red and turning on C-dot fluorescence. Due to the difference in steric hindrance effects and coordination capability between the GSH and the competitive biothiols, discriminative detection of GSH over Cys/Hcy is successfully realized.

In this section, focus will be put on how to condition C-dots for chemo-/biosensing applications. We will summarize the sensing mechanisms and sensor design first, followed by discussing sensing characteristics to various targets, including cations and anions, small molecules, and biological components such as macromolecules, cells, and bacteria. At the end of this section, the challenges and future directions of C-dot-based sensors will also be covered.

Their unique emission properties render C-dot sensing capabilities for a wide range of analytes, including cations and anions; small molecules; and biological components such as macromolecules, cells, and bacteria. The basic design principle for a C-dot-based sensor is to establish the quantitative relationship between the concentration of different analytes and the C-dot fluorescence change in intensity, lifetime, or wavelength [58]. Several well-established mechanisms have been reported to account for fluorescence change of C-dots upon interaction with analytes, namely, photoinduced electron transfer (PET) (Fig. 5.8 A), photoinduced charge transfer (PCT) (Fig. 5.8B) [59], fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Fig. 5.8C), and inner filter effect (IFE) (Fig. 5.8D).

Both PET and PCT are intramolecular processes, during which a complex is formed between the electron donor and the acceptor. In a PET process, the complex formation between C-dots and analytes leads to the internal redox reaction between the excited state of C-dots and analytes, thus quenching the fluorescence of C-dots (Fig. 5.8A). In contrast, PCT is a fluorescence promotion process triggered by charge transfer in the complex formed between C-dots and analytes. And the complex formation results in a change in electron energy levels and also fluorescence signals (Fig. 5.8B).

Different to PET and PCT, IFE and FRET are intermolecular processes, involving at least two independent molecules. IFE results from the absorption of the excitation or emission light by absorbers and leads to the decrease of chromophore fluorescence (Fig. 5.8D). FRET refers to a distance-dependent energy transfer through nonradiative dipole-dipole coupling (Fig. 5.8C). Besides the distance between the donor and the acceptor, the degree of overlap between the donor's emission spectrum and the acceptor's absorption spectrum also determines FRET efficiency. FRET is a dynamic quenching dominated process, where FL lifetime decreases along with the increase of quencher concentrations. In contrast, FL lifetime in IFE-based quenching process is independent on total intensity.

Up to now, there are several classical strategies that have been extensively employed to condition C-dots for sensing and imaging with nice specificity and sensitivity. The simplest and most straightforward one is taking advantages of the direct interaction of analytes with C-dots. Some analytes, especially heavy metal ions, can strongly coordinate with the surface functional groups on C-dot surface, thus changing emission properties of C-dots. Because the functional groups on C-dot surface can be finely tuned via tailoring the synthesis precursors, C-dots have been successfully conditioned for sensing various heavy metal ions such as Cu2+, Hg2+, and Fe3+. For example, Fe3+ can strongly coordinate with the oxygen functional groups on the surface of C-dots, thus quenching C-dot fluorescence due to IFE. Based on this phenomenon, our research group develops a sensitive nanosensing platform toward Fe3+ determination in human serum [61]. In addition, the increase of pH value can induce the dissociation of carboxylic, amino, and hydroxyl groups, thus changing the surface charge status of C-dots and also its emission properties. Therefore, C-dots can also be directly used as pH sensors [62]. Furthermore, oxidant is another typical analyte for C-dot-based sensors that employ the direct interaction strategy, because the emission properties of C-dots can be altered by changing its surface oxidation degree [6]. For example, C-dots have been successfully conditioned for sensing potassium permanganate and hydrogen peroxide that can oxidize C-dot surface, thus changing its emission [63, 64].

However, the specificity of C-dot-based sensor is expected to be further improved by surface conjugation with highly specific recognition elements, such as organic molecules and biomolecules. Therefore, the second strategy is based on surface engineering of C-dots with specific recognition elements. The specific recognition elements can bind the targeted molecules and restrict them to a close range of C-dots with a higher affinity than direct interaction strategy, further changing C-dot emission properties via IFE or FRET. For example, 4′-(aminomethylphenyl)-2,2′:6′,2″-terpyridine (AE-TPY) that can selectively respond to the change of H+ is functionalized onto the surface of C-dots via carbodiimide chemistry under the catalysis of EDC/NHS. The C-dot-based probe has been successfully demonstrated for monitoring pH gradients in a broad pH range of 6.0–8.5 with high sensitivity and specificity [65]. Another advantage of this strategy is that NPs can transport the specific recognition elements neglecting their intrinsic solubility [66]. For example, most of the quinoline-based Zn2+ probes still suffer from poor solubility and bad cell permeability. However, conjugating quinoline-based Zn2+-specific receptors onto the surface of C-dots results in excellent water-soluble and good cell-permeable nanoprobes for quantitation and imaging of Zn2+ with high sensitivity and specificity [50].

The third one always involves a stable FRET system consisting of C-dots and other fluorophores or quenchers. The presence of the targeted analytes can change the FRET efficiency through three different ways: (1) to break the linkage between C-dots and conjugated fluorophores or quenchers (Fig. 5.9 A) [57, 67], (2) to establish the linkage between C-dots and other fluorophores or quenchers [68, 70], and (3) to react with the linked fluorophores or quenchers [69, 71]. For example, Cai and coworkers developed a homogeneous FRET assay for transgenic soybean detection using cauliflower mosaic virus 35s promoter as the specific target. In this study, ssDNA probes that can partially hybridize with the targeted DNA are functionalized onto the surface of C-dots (donor) and silver NPs (AgNPs and acceptor), respectively. Then, the presence of the targeted DNA can bring C-dots and AgNPs into close proximity via DNA hybridization to induce the highly efficient FRET and the significant decrease of C-dot fluorescence (Fig. 5.9B) [68]. In the FRET-based probe developed by You and Wu for α-glucosidase inhibitor screening, C-dots and cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanoflakes are employed as energy donor and acceptor, respectively. The presence of α-glucosidase can catalyze the hydrolysis of l-ascorbic acid-2-O-α-d-glucopyranosyl (AAG) to release ascorbic acids (AA). AA can rapidly reduce CoOOH nanoflakes to Co2+ and thus recover C-dot fluorescence due to the change of FRET efficiency (Fig. 5.9C) [69].

Owing to their built-in correction for environmental effects, ratiometric strategy basing on the ratio of fluorescence intensities at two or more wavelengths is another popular choice to condition C-dots for sensing and targeted imaging [72, 73]. For example, C-dots have been reported to be wrapped with tetrakis(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin (TCPP) to develop a ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe for sensing and imaging of Cu2+. In this design, red emission of TCPP centered at 655nm can be specifically quenched by Cu2+, while green emission of the C-dots centered at 485nm remains constant and acts as internal reference, thus providing a ratiometric fluorescence signal (F TCPP /F C-dots) for Cu2+ quantitation [72]. Similarly, blue-emitting C-dots serving as internal reference and green-emitting CdTe/Cds quantum dots (QDs) acting as the response signal label are encapsulated into molecularly imprinted mesoporous silica to develop a sensor for diniconazole (DNZ) detection using ratiometric fluorescence. The presence of DNZ can specifically quench the green fluorescence of QDs accompanying with a visual green-to-blue color transition [73].

Due to their importance in the environment, food, and biological systems and the availability of various recognition elements, metal ions especially Fe3+, Cu2+, and Hg2+ are favorite targets of C-dot-based sensors. The simplest and most straightforward direct interaction strategy has been extensively employed to condition C-dots for metal ion sensing. For example, the direct and strong coordination between Fe3+ and the phenolic hydroxyl groups of C-dots induces the quench of C-dot emission via a PET mechanism in which an electron in the excited state of C-dots partially transfers to the d orbital of Fe3+ [74]. Through tailoring the synthesis precursors, chemical groups on C-dot surface can be tuned to demonstrate the varying binding properties, thus exhibiting different sensitivities and specificities [61, 74–76].

Doping is another popular way to improve emission properties of C-dots and thus their performance in sensing and imaging [23, 77–79]. It has been reported that the sulfur atom on the edge of S-doped C-dots facilitates fluorescence quenching, because its low electronegativity induces the surface electron density of oxygen atoms around sulfur to form stronger coordination bonds with Fe3+ than undoped C-dots [23, 77]. As a result, the limit of detection (LOD) toward Fe3+ is improved from 18μM for undoped C-dots to 4.2nM for S-doped C-dots [77].

Copper ion is an essential trace element in the human body, and growing evidence suggests the correlations between Cu2+ and some brain diseases such as Parkinson's disease and Alzheimer's disease. Its critical roles in biomedical research highlight the importance of monitoring and visualization of subcellular distribution of Cu2+. Due to its simpleness and straightforwardness, taking advantages of direct interaction between C-dots and targeted ions is still the most popular strategy for sensing Cu2+. Since amine groups can specifically bind Cu2+ to form cupric amine, thus quenching the C-dot fluorescence via IFE mechanism, C-dots with abundant surface amine groups have been synthesized and successfully conditioned for sensing Cu2+ with a LOD lower to 10nM (Fig. 5.10 A) [13, 82]. Since surface groups on pristine C-dots determine the final specificity and sensitivity when using direct interaction strategy, the specificity should be further improved. As expected, determination of Cu2+ with improved specificity has been demonstrated by surface engineering of C-dots with Cu2+ receptor TPEA (Fig. 5.10B) [80]. However, the single-emission fluorescent probes targeting Cu2+ that are operated with a turn-on or turnoff mode still suffer from the easy disturbance by various factors such as probe molecule concentration, environment factors (pH, temperature, and polarity), and photobleaching. To address this issue, Tian and coworkers develop a ratiometric nanoprobe with well-resolved dual emissions. In Tian's design, red-emitting CdSe/ZnS QDs are employed as the internal standard and encapsulated into silica core, and green-emitting C-dots functionalized with a Cu2+-specific recognition element AE-TPEA is employed as response signal label and directly presented on the outer silica shell. The blue emission continuously decreased along with the stepwise addition of Cu2+, whereas the red emission remains constant, resulting in the emission color changing from green to purple and thus enabling visual detection of Cu2+ by the naked eyes [83]. Similar ratiometric strategy has also been employed for Cu2+ sensing by Shangguan and coworkers, where C-dots were conjugated onto the surface of rhodamine B-doped silica nanoparticles as response signal label and the red fluorescence of rhodamine B was employed as the internal standard (Fig. 5.10C) [81].

Hg2+ is also the extensively investigated target because of its toxic feature toward health such as mitosis impairment, DNA damage, and permanent damage to the central nervous system. Similar to the detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+, direct interaction strategy has been extensively employed to condition C-dots for Hg2+ sensing. A typical example is presented by Sun et al., where the direct interaction between Hg2+ and the carboxylic group of C-dots exhibits strong affinity, thus resulting in sensitive and selective Hg2+ detection with a LOD of 0.23nM [84]. Zhao and coworkers present a unique C-dot-based nanoprobe for Hg2+ detection. The as-prepared C-dots exhibit two distinct emission peaks at ~470 and ~678nm under a single excitation. Interestingly, Hg2+ can only remarkably quench the emission at 678nm, while the emission at 470nm was slightly changed, thus presenting a ratiometric fluorescence signal for Hg2+ detection [85]. However, after surface functionalization of C-dots with dithiothreitol (DTT), the LOD is found to be as low as 18pM, because of the stronger affinity between thiols and Hg2+ [86]. Taking advantages of the specific and strong interactions between thymine base and Hg2+, a FRET-based nanosensor that consists of DNA-functionalized C-dots as donor and graphene oxide as acceptor is developed for Hg2+ detection with a LOD of 2.6nM. The presence of Hg2+ can break the linkage between DNA-functionalized C-dots and graphene oxide and recover C-dot fluorescence, due to the formation of T-Hg2+-T duplex [87]. Using as-prepared C-dots as donor and polyaniline (PANI) as acceptor, another FRET-based nanosensor is also successfully developed and demonstrated for Hg2+ detection with an LOD of 0.8nM [88].

Besides Fe3+, Cu2+, and Hg2+, a series of other metal ions such as Zn2+, Pb2+, Cr6+, Ag+, Au3+, and K+ have also been determined using fluorescent C-dots. Actually, in recent years, extensive research focus has been emphasized on the synthesis of C-dots using different precursors and methods and conditioning them for sensing and targeted imaging through different mechanisms. The direct interaction strategy is always the favorite choice for the development of C-dot-based nanosensor, because of its convenience and simpleness. For example, C-dots have been successfully conditioned for Pb2+ detection with a LOD of 4.6nM, because the strong interaction between carboxylate, organo-nitrogen groups on C-dot surface, and Pb2+ results in the formation of C-dots/Pb2+ complexes that can quench C-dot fluorescence via IFE mechanism [89]. Based on the direct interaction strategy and IFE mechanism, Cr6+ [90] and Au3+ [91] can also be detected using C-dots with LOD of 0.01 and 0.48μM, respectively. Different to fluorescence turnoff mechanism, fluorescence turn-on mechanism has also been employed for sensing Ag+ with a LOD of 320nM. The reduction of Ag+ to Ag0 on the C-dot surface and simultaneous oxidation of C-dots can enhance the C-dot emission intensity [92].

Few examples can be found for sensing and imaging Pb2+, Cr6+, Ag+, and Au3+ through functionalizing C-dots with their specific receptors, possibly due to the lack of such receptors. Anyway, in our group, Zn2+-specific receptors have been functionalized onto the surface of blue-emitting C-dots to develop a Zn2+ nanoprobe (Fig. 5.11 A). The specific coordination between Zn2+ receptors and Zn2+ triggers a green emission. Therefore, the emission color of solution containing C-dot changed from blue to green continuously in the presence of Zn2+, enabling the Zn2+ sensing by the naked eyes. More importantly, due to the emission color change, the interference of spontaneous fluorescence from complicated biological matrix can be avoided. Rapid detection of Zn2+ in aqueous media with a LOD of 6.4nM and intracellular imaging of Zn2+ with high efficiency have been achieved successfully [50]. In addition, a FRET system consisting of amino-terminated C-dots as donor and graphene oxide nanosheet functionalized with 18-crown-6 ether (18C6E) via CH-π interactions as acceptor that can specifically recognize K+ is established for K+ sensing (Fig. 5.11B). The competition between K+ and amine for the binding site of 18C6E results in the FRET efficiency change and thus is being employed for K+ determination with a LOD of 10μM [93]. A unique ECL sensor using N-doped C-dots as luminophores, specific DNA as recognition elements, and Pd-Au hexoctahedrons (Pd@Au HOHs) as enhancers was developed by Yuan and Chai for sensing of intracellular Pb2+ with a LOD of 0.33ng mL−1 (Fig. 5.11C) [94]. In this study, N-doped C-dots were in situ electropolymerized on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). Then, GCE was further immobilized with AgNPs and followed by amino-modified capture DNA (T1) via Ag-N bonds. Through DNA hybridization, Pd@Au HOHs were introduced to catalyze the ECL signal from N-doped C-dots. However, Pb2+ can couple with DNA sequence on Pd@Au HOHs to form a Pb2+-stabilized G-quadruplex (G4) structure, thus quenching the ECL signal.

As for anion sensing using C-dots, there are only a few successful examples most of which are based on the recovery of C-dot fluorescence previously quenched by cations. The specific binding of anions and cations induces the release of C-dots from C-dot/cation complexes. A typical example is presented by Huang et al., where the Eu3+ quenched fluorescence of C-dots is recovered by phosphate ions (Pi) due to the stronger affinity of Eu3+ to PO4 3− than to carboxylate groups [95]. The similar fluorescence off-on systems such as (Pb2+-P2O7 4−) [96], (Cu2+-CN−) [97], (Cu2+-H2S) [98], (Cu2+-S2−) [99], and (Ni2+-F−) [100] have also been designed for sensing different anions. Following the study of our group, a dual-mode nanosensor consisting of amino-terminated C-dots and carboxyl-terminated AuNPs is developed for sensing SCN−. The stronger affinity between Au-SCN− than that between Au-N induces the change of distance between AuNPs and C-dots, thus presenting a solution color change from blue to red and the recovery of C-dot fluorescence.

In addition, the direct interaction strategy is also successfully employed for sensing anions such as ClO− and Cl− [101, 102]. Through destroying C-dot surface passivation layer by oxidation of its surface hydroxyl groups, ClO− can quench the C-dot fluorescence [101]. Using the same mechanism but N-S-doped C-dots, a nanosensor toward Cl− detection has also been successfully developed [102].

Due to their frequent involvement in the reversible redox reactions and cellular functions such as signal transduction and xenobiotic metabolism, sensing and imaging of GSH and cysteine (Cys) herein play an important role in disease diagnosis and fundamental biomedical research. Employing the fluorescence off-on mechanism, GSH determination has been realized through recovering C-dot fluorescence from various systems, including (C-dots-Au3+) [91], (C-dots-Cu2+) [104], (C-dots-AuNPs) [57], and (C-dots-MnO2 nanosheet) [105], while Cys detection has been accomplished using (C-dots-Hg2+) [106] and (C-dots-Ag+) [107] systems. For example, GSH can recover C-dot fluorescence that is quenched by Au3+ due to the stronger affinity of Au3+ to GSH than to carboxyl and amine groups, based on which a nanosensor is developed for GSH detection with a LOD of 2.02μM [91]. The analytic performance of C-dot-based nanosensor toward GSH can be further improved by employing FRET-based platforms. It has been reported that stable FRET systems consisting of C-dots as donor and metal NPs such as AuNPs or MnO2 nanosheet as acceptor have been successfully conditioned for GSH sensing. The detection limit for FRET platforms of C-dots-AuNPs and C-dots-MnO2 nanosheet is significantly improved to 50nM [57] and 22nM [105], respectively.

Because the emission properties of C-dots can be changed by tuning its surface oxidation degree [6], strong oxidants such as reactive oxygen and nitrogen species (RONS) that play critical roles in physiological and pathological processes can be detected using C-dots via direct interaction strategy [106–108]. For example, Jelinek and coworkers prepared C-dots through hydrothermal treatment of aminoguanidine hydrochloride and CA. The retained aminoguanidine on as-prepared C-dot surface can specifically react with NO to generate a diazonium ion that in turn undergoes a diazo coupling reaction to form a polymeric chain of C-dots. In this reaction, N2 is released, and azo dye is formed on the surface of C-dots, which therefore presents both the fluorescence and colorimetric signals for specific NO sensing [106]. In another example, H2O2 can reduce the size of C-dots through oxidizing its surface and thus enhancing the fluorescence of C-dots possibly due to giving rise to confinement of emissive energy traps. However, the detection limit to H2O2 is 5μM [107].

Generally, the specificity and sensitivity of C-dot-based sensor is expected to be further improved by surface conjugation with highly specific recognition elements. Therefore, using well-established carbodiimide chemistry under the catalysis of EDC and NHS, H2O2 recognition elements such as diphenylphosphine moiety [109] and NP-BE [110] were covalently conjugated onto C-dot surface for highly specific H2O2 sensing. As anticipated, the detection limit for receptor-functionalized C-dots is significantly improved to 84nM [109] and 0.5μM [110], respectively. It is worthy to mention that specific imaging H2O2 is also realized in living cell and zebra fish larvae [110]. NO recognition element is also covalently conjugated onto C-dot surface to develop a FRET-based ratiometric NO nanosensor. The NO recognition element is delicately designed by linking a naphthalimide with an o-phenylenediamine group. Since o-phenylenediamine moiety can quench the fluorescence of naphthalimide moiety, the functionalized C-dots emit intrinsic blue fluorescence with no FRET. While, NO can react with o-phenylenediamine to generate an electron-poor aryltriazole moiety and trigger strong FRET between C-dots and naphthalimide moiety, thus recovering the fluorescence of the naphthalimide moiety (Fig. 5.13 A). Therefore, the nanosensor can detect NO in aqueous media with a LOD of 3nM and trace NO in living cells [111]. Similarly, sensing and imaging of superoxide (O2 •−) with high accuracy and excellent specificity are realized by covalently conjugating O2 •− recognition element hydroethidine (HE) onto C-dot surface via ratiometric strategy. The nanosensor exhibits a broad dynamic linear range of 5×10−7–1.4×10−4 M with a LOD of 100nM (Fig. 5.13B) [112].

It is well known that glucose oxidase (GOx) can catalyze the oxidation of glucose to produce a stoichiometric amount of H2O2. Therefore, employing GOx as glucose-recognition elements, C-dots that can specifically response to H2O2 can be conditioned for glucose sensing through determination of the produced H2O2 [28, 112–114]. For example, using B-doped C-dots as specific H2O2 sensor, an assay system for glucose quantification with a detection limit of 8.0μM has been successfully demonstrated [28]. In a recent example, Yin and coworkers design and prepare C-dots supported on AgNP (C-dot/AgNP) nanocomplexes for glucose detection. In their design, C-dot fluorescence will be quenched by AgNPs via surface plasmon-enhanced energy transfer (SPEET) but can be restored by H2O2 through etching the AgNPs to silver ions and thus releasing C-dots from the C-dot/AgNP nanocomplexes (Fig. 5.14 A). Employing GOx as glucose-recognition elements, the nanocomplexes allow the quantitation of glucose with a LOD of 1.39μM [114].

Another frequently employed glucose-recognition element is boronic acid motif that exhibits strong affinity toward glucose by forming cyclic boronate ester through binding to 1,2- and 1,3-diols [58, 115–117]. A typical example of the boronic acid-functionalized C-dots is presented by Xia et al. Phenylboronic acid whose benzene ring is a good carbon source and contains boronic acid group and hydrothermal carbonization is employed as the precursor and the synthesis method, respectively. Glucose can specifically induce the cross-linking of C-dots via covalent bonds, thus leading to a linear fluorescence quenching in the range of 9–900μM with a LOD of 1.5μM (Fig. 5.14B) [115]. Different to fluorescence on-off mechanism, Qiu et al. present a glucose sensor using N,N′-4,4′-bis(benzyl-2-boronic acid)bipyridinium dibromide (BBV) as glucose-recognition element and C-dots as reporter through fluorescence off-on mechanism. Electrostatic attraction between negatively charged C-dots and positively charged BBV results in the formation of a ground-state complex C-dots/BBV, leading to weak fluorescence emission. Glucose can convert the boronic acids to tetrahedral anionic glucoboronate esters, which can effectively neutralize the net charge of the cationic bipyridinium, leading to a linear fluorescence increase in the range of 1–60mM [117].

The applications of C-dot-based sensors have also been extended for food safety inspection including the detection of veterinary drug and pesticide residue [118–128], illegal additives [129–131], and nutrients such as vitamins [69, 132, 133]. Direct interactions between analytes and C-dots induce fluorescence change that is still the most frequently employed strategy [118–120, 127, 132]. Different to most examples of direct interactions in which C-dot fluorescence is always quenched by analytes via PET or FRET mechanism, norfloxacin can enhance C-dot fluorescence, possibly due to their hydrogen-bond interactions [118]. The detection of pesticides, especially organophosphorus (OP) compounds always involves an enzyme such as acetylcholinesterase (AChE) [122, 123] or butyrylcholinesterase (BChE) [128]. For example, a FRET system consisting of C-dots as donor and AuNPs as acceptor is developed for OP detection. In this design, BChE can hydrolyze acetylthiocholine (ATC) to produce thiocholine, which in turn causes AuNP aggregation and destroy the FRET system, resulting in the recovery of C-dot fluorescence. While in the presence of OP, the catalytic activity of BChE is inhibited, which cause the disaggregation of AuNPs and the enhanced FRET efficiency, resulting in the decrease of C-dot fluorescence [128]. In another example for OP detection, two different enzymes are involved, namely, AChE and choline oxidase (ChOx). In that design, N-S-doped C-dots that can specifically response to H2O2 are conditioned for OP detection. AChE can catalyze the hydrolysis of acetylcholine chloride (Ach) to produce choline that can be further oxidized by ChOx to generate H2O2; therefore, OP detection can be realized through determining H2O2 [126]. Through a one-step hydrothermal treatment of lactose, vitamin B12, and H3PO4, C-dots capped with vitamin B12 (VB12), C-dots@VB12, are prepared and exhibit two emissions centered at 417 and 550nm. And carbofuran phenol-3-keto can selectively quench the C-dot emission at 550nm, thus presenting a ratiometric fluorescence signal for 3-keto detection [125].

Illegal additives such as melamine and clenbuterol are also popular analytes for food safety inspection that have been measured using fluorescent C-dots. Using the similar FRET system consisting of C-dots as donor and AuNPs as acceptor, melamine and clenbuterol have been determined, because they can change the FRET efficiency through stronger interaction of Au-N bonds than electrostatic interaction between AuNPs and C-dots [129, 130]. Recently, Li and coworkers design an assay for fluorescent and colorimetric detection of melamine in raw milk using C-dots and AgNPs. In this assay, the hydroxyl-rich C-dots serve as the reductant and stabilizer for AgNP synthesis and a fluorescent reporter. The formation of AgNPs can induce color change of the solution and the quench of C-dot fluorescence. The presence of melamine can inhibit the formation of AgNPs, resulting in visual color changes and increased fluorescence [131]. Nutrients in food such as vitamins can also be measured by fluorescent C-dots [69, 132, 133]. It has been reported that VB12 can quench C-dot fluorescence through direct interactions and thus had been successfully detected [132]. Through changing the efficiency of C-dot-based FRET systems via the reaction with linked quenchers, monitoring of cerebral AA in brain microdialysate has also been successfully realized [133].

Though still limited, due to their vital roles in biological science and biomedical diagnostics, there are significantly growing studies on conditioning C-dots for sensing biomacromolecules such as enzymes, antibodies, and oligonucleotide, especially in recent 5 years. For example, alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is an important biomarker for assisting diagnostics of a series of disease, including liver dysfunction, bone disease, and breast and prostatic cancer [60, 134–137]. The sensing strategy for ALP always involves the specific reaction with their substrates such as pyrophosphate (PPi) anions [134, 136], adenosine triphosphate (ATP) [135], and p-nitrophenylphosphate (PNPP) [60, 137], whose products can change C-dot fluorescence. Feng's group carries out a series of research work for ALP detection using C-dots. The electrostatic interaction between metal ions such as Cu2+ [134], Ce3+ [135], and Ni2+ [136] and carboxylate groups on C-dot surface can induce the aggregation of C-dots, resulting in C-dot fluorescence quench. Under the catalytic hydrolysis of ALP, PPi and ATP can be readily transformed to PO4 3−, which in turn restores C-dot fluorescence due to stronger affinity of metal ions to PO4 3− than to carboxylate groups (Fig. 5.15 A) [134–136]. Basing on the same strategy, fluorometric assays for the detection of glutathione reductase [138] and acetylcholinesterase [139] activity and screening of their inhibitors have also been successfully developed using C-dots.

Feng and coworkers further functionalize C-dots with hydroxypropyl β-cyclodextrins (β-CD) to prepare β-CD-C-dot nanoprobe for ALP detection. Under the catalysis of ALP, PNPP can be readily transformed to p-nitrophenol (PNP) that can enter the cavity of β-CD moiety owing to host-guest recognition, resulting in C-dot fluorescence quench via a PET process (Fig. 5.15B) [137]. Basing on the same strategy, fluorometric assay for the determination of β-galactosidase activity in vitro and in living cells has also been successfully developed using C-dots [140].

Since PNP exhibits a stronger absorption at 405nm, Wu and coworkers develop a simple fluorescent assay for ALP sensing based on IFE mechanism, through tuning the maximum excitation wavelength of C-dots to 405nm [60]. The same design principle has also been successfully employed for fluorometric detection of α-glucosidase activity and screening of its inhibitors using C-dots [141]. In this assay, 4-nitrophenol that can be specifically released from 4-nitrophenyl-α-d-glucopyranoside (NGP) under the catalysis of α-glucosidase exhibits strong absorption at the wavelength of C-dot maximum excitation, thus affecting the fluorescent signal of C-dots (Fig. 5.15C).

FRET systems are extensively employed for sensing biomolecules, since specific linkage of two complementary biomolecules can precisely control the distance between energy donors and acceptors and subsequently change the FRET efficiency. As discussed previously, there are three different ways to change the efficiency of FRET systems consisting of C-dots and other fluorophores or quenchers: (1) to break the linkage between C-dots and conjugated fluorophores or quenchers, (2) to establish the linkage between C-dots and other fluorophores or quenchers, and (3) to react with the linked fluorophores or quenchers.

Basing on the strategy of reaction with the linked quenchers, FRET-based sensors have been developed for hyaluronidase (HAase) determination. In brief, negatively charged hyaluronic acid (HA) caused the aggregation of positively charged C-dots and neutral red (NR) via electrostatic interactions, resulting in highly efficient FRET from C-dots to NR and red fluorescence emission. However, the presence of HAase can change the FRET efficiency via specific enzymolysis of HA, resulting in the fluorescence color changing from red to green-yellow and a reliable ratiometric fluorescence signal for sensing HAase [71]. In addition, basing on the strategy of establishing the linkage between C-dots and fluorophores, FRET-based sensors have been developed for the detection of CaMV35S promoter in transgenic soybean [70], CA125 tumor marker [68], and hemoglobin [142].

Basing on the strategy of breaking the linkage between C-dots and conjugated fluorophores, FRET-based sensors have been developed for the detection of HIV-1 gene [67], thioredoxin reductase (TrxR) [143], and BRCA1 and TK1 genes [144]. In a recent example, a FRET system consisting of C-dots and its surface-conjugated naphthalimide moiety via a disulfide linkage is successfully demonstrated for monitoring the activity of TrxR. The presence of TrxR can cut the disulfide bonds and consequently change the FRET efficiency by the release of the naphthalimide moiety, resulting in the fluorescence color changing from orange-yellow to blue and a reliable ratiometric fluorescence signal for sensing TrxR [143].

Yang and coworkers present a delicately designed assay for simultaneously sensing breast cancer 1 (BRCA1) and thymidine kinase (TK1) genes. In their design, AuNPs conjugated with ssDNA that is complementary to both BRCA1 and TK1 are employed as acceptors. C-dots conjugated with ssDNA that is partially complementary to ssDNA on AuNPs and hairpin structure that is also partially complementary to ssDNA on AuNPs are employed as fluorescence reporters. As illustrated in Fig. 5.16 , in the absence of any targets, both C-dots and HS hybridize with ssDNA on AuNPs, thus presenting only fluorescence of Cy3 by forming FRET between AuNPs and C-dots and breaking hairpin structure. The presence of BRCA1 gene can restore C-dot fluorescence via changing FRET efficiency between AuNPs and C-dots, thus presenting both fluorescence of Cy3 and C-dots. In the same way, the presence of TK1 can release hairpin structure and quench Cy3 fluorescence, thus presenting no fluorescence at all. The presence of BRCA1 and TK1 genes can change FRET efficiency between AuNPs and C-dots and simultaneously release hairpin structure, thus presenting only fluorescence of C-dots [144]. In addition, based on the phenomena of aggregation-induced C-dot fluorescence quench and CEA-specific aptamer, quantitation of CEA in a range from 1ngmL−1 to 0.5mgmL−1 with a LOD of 0.3ngmL−1 was realized [145]. DNA-functionalized C-dots have also been successfully employed as ECL signal reporter for miRNA-21 detection with a LOD of 10aM [146] and as fluorescent signal reporter for lateral-flow DNA detection with a LOD of 0.4fM [147].

Detection and assessment of bacteria play an important role in many areas such as diagnosis and treatment of infectious disease and drug development. Because of its simple and inexpensive preparation, C-dots hold great promise for the detection and targeted imaging of bacteria. For example, Chen and Wu prepare fluorescent C-dots via one-step hydrothermal carbonization of bacteria and use the as-prepared C-dots for live/dead differentiation and viability assessment of microbes [148]. Interestingly, C-dots prepared via one-step hydrothermal treatment of papaya powder by Wang and coworkers exhibit significant fluorescence enhancement effect upon interaction with Escherichia coli (E. coli) O157:H7 with a linear range of 105–108 CFUmL−1 and an LOD of 9.5×104 CFUmL−1 [75].

Surface functionalization of C-dots with specific recognition elements can always improve the specificity of C-dots toward sensing and targeted imaging of bacteria and virus. An early example demonstrated that the spectral position and intensity of the hydrocarbon chain-functionalized C-dot fluorescence depend upon bacterial species, making distinguishing among different strains possible [149]. Through carbonization of ammonium citrate in solid form in the presence of mannose, Huang and coworkers synthesize mannose-modified C-dots (Man-C-dots) for quantitation and labeling of E. coli [150]. Besides this one-step synthesis method, Huang and coworkers also report a two-step dry-heating reaction for the Man-C-dot synthesis, where pristine C-dots are firstly prepared by heating solid-state ammonium citrate followed by being heated with mannose [151]. The specific binding of the mannose units to E. coli enables its quantitative detection. The Man-C-dots synthesized via two-step method exhibit higher efficiency in the detection of E. coli than that synthesized via one-step method, as evidenced by the LOD for E. coli quantitation that is 100 and 450CFUmL−1, respectively [150, 151]. Yang et al. established a new method for detecting Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) basing on surface functionalization of C-dots with vancomycin (Van-C-dots) that can bind to the terminal peptide of d-Ala-d-Ala on the cell wall of the Gram-positive bacterium via hydrogen bonds. Due to the specific interactions between vancomycin and bacteria, Van-C-dots will aggregate on bacteria surfaces, resulting in C-dot fluorescence quench [152].

ce:section.ce:section-title: C-Dots for Bioimaging and Therapy Applications
ce:section.ce:para: The extraordinary properties of C-dots, such as unique photoluminescence, excellent biocompatibility, and water dispersibility, have opened new avenues for their advanced application in cell labeling, bioimaging, and drug delivery. In this section, we critically review recent advances in the application of C-dots for bioimaging, therapy, and theranostics.

Biomedical applications are the most promising applications of C-dots. These fluorescent nanomaterials offer a robust tool for exploring the challenges in biomedical fields, especially in diagnosis, bioimaging, drug or gene delivery, and cancer therapy. It has been already shown that C-dots fulfill the two critical criteria for clinical use: low toxicity and rapid excretion. The large surface area of C-dots and the availability of abundant functional groups on the surface allow for the preparation of multimodal probes and therapeutic conjugates [153].

As we know, the sensitivity, resolution, and versatility of fluorescence microscopy mainly depend on the properties of the fluorescent probes. Thus, choosing suitable probes is significant for bioimaging applications. During the past few years, urged by the demands in optical imaging field, considerable researches have been directed to exploit the PL properties of C-dots. At first, C-dots surpass organic dyes in photostability and high quantum yield. In addition, due to the merit of excellent biocompatibility, C-dots are overtaking semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) to become a potential fluorescent nanocarrier or nanoprobe. More importantly, some latest reported C-dots could exhibit fluorescent emission in the near-infrared (NIR) region that can avoid the autofluorescence from organisms, minimizing the signal-to-noise ratio and enhancing the tissue penetration [154]. Furthermore, now, a very promising research direction is to expand the imaging scale and modal of C-dots, so the multiscale and multimodal imaging of C-dots is emphatically discussed here.

The first report on using C-dots as fluorescent labels in cells was demonstrated by Sun et al. [2] to stain Caco-2 cells. The confocal microscopy image revealed that the cells have internalized the C-dots into the cytoplasm mainly via endocytosis pathway (Fig. 5.17 A). The as-prepared C-dots exhibited a QY of 4%–10% without experiencing any blinking or photobleaching as compared with organic dyes and quantum dots. In the early stage of the C-dot research, most obtained C-dots emit blue or green fluorescence under the excitation, so the imaging could largely be interfered by the autofluorescence from organisms. Searching for a kind of long wavelength emission C-dots is in need of being solved urgently.

The first hope is the excitation-dependent of C-dots. The C-dots synthesized by our group could emit different fluorescence colors under different wavelength excitations (Fig. 5.17B) [51]. However, QY of the red emission here is too low to satisfy the biomedical applications. Hence, some real red-emissive C-dots, two-photon fluorescence, and upconversion photoluminescence of C-dots have been exploited. Though many research groups reported the upconversion C-dots in these years, Wu and coworkers pointed out that most of the upconversion C-dots now reported are not real due to wrong experiments designed [156]. Even so, until today, some achievements have gotten in this direction. For example, Lin et al. firstly reported a near-infrared (NIR) emissive C-dots capable of excitation with an NIR femtosecond pulse laser (850nm). The as-prepared C-dots exhibited a high QY of 16.8% and verified to be biocompatible. Unexpectedly, two-photon fluorescence bioimaging can also be achieved using the NIR-emissive C-dots (Fig. 5.17C) [155]. And Wang and coworkers also prepared C-dots with red emission (from 500 to 800nm with a peak at 640nm) from polythiophene phenylpropionic acid (PPA) [157].

In addition to the abovementioned examples, nonspecific cellular imaging of various cell lines including NIH-3T3 fibroblast cells, human lung cancer (A549), and fungus has also been successfully demonstrated. However, for biomedical applications, targeted imaging should be more useful. Therefore, surface engineering C-dots with a specific recognition element have been extensively exploited for the targeted imaging of specific parts of a cell or a specific cellular type. For example, nuclear localization signal (NLS) peptide and mitochondria-targeted triphosphonium were successfully conjugated onto C-dot surface for imaging of nucleus (Fig. 5.18 A) [158] and mitochondria (Fig. 5.18B) [159], respectively. Interestingly, magnetic field could significantly enhance the cellular uptake efficiency of C-dots because of the presence of Fe3O4 [159].

It has been well established that folate receptors, transferrin receptors, and CD 44 receptor are overexpressed on the surface of cancer cells. Therefore, for targeted imaging of cancer cells, FA, transferrin, and HA that can specifically recognize CD 44 receptor have been extensively exploited for surface engineering C-dots [160–162]. For example, transferrin-functionalized C-dots have been successfully employed for targeted imaging of HeLa cells [160]. FA-functionalized C-dots are capable of distinguishing cancer cells from normal cells, as verified by the fact that HeLa cells (FA-positive cancer cells) exhibit bright fluorescence while NIH-3T3 cells (FA-negative normal cells) do not [161].

In vivo imaging using C-dots are more challenging than cell labeling. In addition to the good biocompatibility and sufficient cell uptake, C-dot should also preserve some other properties for in vivo applications, such as low nonspecific protein adsorption, hemocompatibility, and rapid extraction from the body with sufficient delivery to the targeted tissue [153, 163]. The first example of in vivo imaging applications of C-dots was demonstrated using mice as a model by Sun and coworkers [164]. Enlightened by this study, profound efforts have been paid over recent years for conditioning C-dots for in vivo imaging using various animal models such as mice and zebra fish.

In order to avoid autofluorescence from organisms and increase tissue penetration without much tissue absorbance and scattering, NIR imaging is among the most sought after fluorescence imaging techniques. For example, in vivo NIR fluorescence imaging using C-dots has been successfully demonstrated through subcutaneously injection to mice by Tao and coworkers. As illustrated in this study, much better signal-to-noise ratio has been achieved using longer wavelength excitations [165]. Interestingly, Zheng and coworkers reported that l-aspartic-acid-derived C-dots without any modification could easily pass the blood-brain barrier (BBB) after intravenous injection via the tail of C6 glioma-bearing mice. As shown in Fig. 5.19 A, stronger FL signal of C-dots was observed in the glioma site 5min after injection [166]. In most of the studies, C-dots are accumulated in various parts of the body without specificity, such as the liver, kidney, stomach, testes, brain, spleen, bladder, and bone [163]. However, high degree of tissue specificity is an essential prerequisite for molecular imaging. It is obvious that the lack of tissue specificity severely restricts the bioimaging application of C-dots.

Zebra fish is another well-established animal model for biomedical research, because of its optical clarity and especially the ability to manipulate gene activity with superb temporal and spatial control [163]. Yin and coworkers revealed the fate of C-dots in zebra fish. This study confirmed that C-dots can enter into zebra fish through two possible routes: skin absorption and swallowing. More importantly, this study disclosed the distribution of C-dots in the same fish at its different development stages [168]. An interesting example presented by Leblanc et al. demonstrated the binding of C-dots with low QY to calcified bone structures of live zebra fish larvae with high affinity and selectivity (Fig. 5.19B). Binding of C-dots to calcified bone structures resulted in a strong enhancement of fluorescence, making early bone loss detection possible [167].

Since it is difficult for an individual imaging modality alone to achieve quick and accurate data collection and disease diagnosis due to its inherent limitations, there is a great demand for developing novel imaging probes that cooperatively combine multiple imaging modalities for better sensitivity, resolution, and tissue penetration. As a novel nanomaterial with remarkable advantages including favorable optical properties, green synthesis and ease of surface modification, and excellent biocompatibility, C-dots present itself as a superior framework for developing multimodal nanoprobes for bioimaging [37–41, 51, 158].

Photoacoustic (PA) imaging is a hybrid imaging modality whose merits are deep tissue penetration and high spatial resolution. Therefore, it would be advantageous to integrate FL and PA imaging into a single probe due to the combination of deeper tissue penetration of PA imaging and higher sensitivity of FL imaging [154]. Interestingly, C-dots have also been validated as a good PA contrast agent. For example, red-emissive C-dots prepared from PPA exhibit high photon absorbance and efficiency to convert light to heat, presenting themselves as an ideal contrast agent for PA imaging [157]. Wang et al. exploited the honey-derived C-dots as contrast agent for PA imaging of sentinel lymph nodes (SLN) to monitor the cancer staging in a real-time manner. The pristine C-dots exhibited a diameter of 7±3nm and could illuminate SLN within ~2min (Fig. 5.20 ), enabling the direct identification and resection of SLN or collection of tissue samples [169].

FL/MR probes are the most successfully implemented in clinic among all multimodal imaging probes, because MRI benefits surgical planning due to its excellent 3-D spatial resolution while FL imaging provides visual clues for the surgeon during the procedure due to its single-cell sensitivity. Combining Gd (III) and fluorescent C-dots has proved to be a promising route for the synthesis of C-dot-based FL/MR imaging probes. Our group successfully synthesized C-dot-based FL/MR imaging probes via two different strategies: step-by-step surface functionalization [51] and one-pot hydrothermal doping [40].
