The prepared C-dot-based dual-modal FL/MR imaging probes not only exhibited excellent cell-membrane permeability and biocompability but also significantly improved FL and MR signals.

Another route for the synthesis of C-dot-based FL/MR imaging probes is to integrate magnetic Fe3O4 NPs into fluorescent C-dots via hydrothermal decomposition of organic precursors using Fe3O4 NPs as seeds [170, 171] or by depositing Fe3O4 NPs on graphene oxide framework [172, 173]. Unexpectedly, in addition to FL/MR imaging modes, carbon-iron oxide hybrid quantum dots (C-Fe3O4 QDs) that are prepared via a one-pot hydrothermal approach employing poly(γ-glutamic acid) as both the precursor and stabilizer show observable X-ray attenuation effect for computed tomography (CT) imaging. Iodine, a widely used CT contrast agent, has been successfully doped into C-dots via a bottom-up hydrothermal carbonization process for preparing FL/CT dual-modal imaging probes [174].

Another important biomedical application of C-dots is its therapeutic potential, especially their capability to act as nanoscale carriers. Thanks to the phases of sp2- and sp3-hybridized carbon nanostructures and abundant surface functional groups, inorganic, polymeric, organic, or biological species can be easily functionalized onto C-dots via various approaches such as π-π stacking, physisorption, hydrophobic interaction, electrostatic attraction, or chemical modifications. The functionalization diversity enables C-dots to be an excellent delivery vehicle for genes, anticancer drugs, and photosensitizers [12].

PEI has been extensively exploited for biological purposes such as nucleic acid delivery and gene therapy, owing to their highly positive charges that compact DNA and siRNA into complexes to facilitate cell-membrane permeation. In the meantime, PEI has been proved to be a good surface passivation reagent for C-dot preparation. Rapid synthesis of PEI-terminated C-dots (PEI-C-dots) for gene delivery and bioimaging has been achieved by Liu et al. using one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis of glycerol with PEI25k. These PEI-C-dots exhibit not only excellent water solubility, bright multicolor fluorescence, and low cytotoxicity but also higher efficiency to mediate gene transfection in COS-7 and HepG2 cells than pristine PEI25k [175]. It has been well established that gene transfection efficiency and biocompatibility of lower-molecular-weight PEI can be improved through modification with a hydrophobic moiety such as alkyl-PEI2k. Therefore, Wang and coworkers prepared PEI-C-dots by using alkyl-PEI2k for surface passivation and gene delivery and demonstrated successful gene expression and gene silencing in a xenografted tumor mouse model by using PEI-C-dots-pDNA and PEI-C-dots-siRNA complexes, respectively [176]. Similarly, branched polyethylenimine (MW=600Da) was also used to passivate C-dots and further encapsulate multiple siRNAs (EGFR and cyclin B1) for lung cancer therapy. As shown in Fig. 5.21 , through receptor-mediated endocytosis, folate-conjugated and PEI-passivated C-dots can specifically accumulate in lung cancer cells, leading to better gene silencing and anticancer effects [177].

Various therapeutic molecules such as chlorine e6 (Ce6), protoporphyrin IX, doxorubicin (DOX), and zinc phthalocyanine (ZnPc) have been proved to exhibit nice anticancer effects. However, most of these therapeutic molecules still suffer from low water solubility and therapeutic index, severe toxicity, and side effects. Nanoscale drug delivery systems (DDS) present themselves a promising solution, since NPs can eliminate unnecessary contact of drugs with healthy cells and can transport the drugs neglecting their intrinsic solubility. More importantly, nanoscale DDS can significantly improve anticancer efficacy of the carried drugs, because of their “passive” accumulation in tumors induced by enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect and/or their “active” targeting tumors induced by targeting ligand for overexpressed receptors in tumor [12].

Due to their versatile synthesis and surface functionalization, C-dots have been extensively used for anticancer drug delivery. Through covalent conjugations, hydrophobic interactions, or π-π stacking, anticancer drugs can be loaded onto C-dots. A FRET-based C-dot DDS was synthesized by a direct surface coupling strategy and was successfully demonstrated to monitor drug release in real-time fashion [178]. First, amino-terminated PEG (PEG-NH2) was modified to C-dots, and the resultant composite was further conjugated with the targeting ligand FA. Afterward, DOX was adsorbed onto C-dots via electrostatic interaction and stacking to form a conjugate. In this DDS, FRET will occur between C-dots and DOX, resulting in the decrease of C-dot green emission and the increase of DOX red emission. As the DDS gets into the tumors, the FRET efficiency will decrease because of the release of DOX from C-dot surface, leading to the recovery of C-dot fluorescence. Therefore, the DOX release process can be directly monitored and quantificated by C-dot fluorescence [178].

Controlled release of anticancer drugs at the site of interest can avoid severe side effects and improve their anticancer efficacy. It's well known that the extracellular microenvironment of tumor cells is more acidic (6.0–6.5) than that of blood (7.4), and the pH value of the intracellular lysosomes and endosomes is even lower (pH 5.0–5.5). This enables the design of drug-controlled release system based on pH. For example, Dong and Choi designed a C-dot-based nanocarrier for DOX delivery and controlled release triggered by pH. DOX was loaded onto C-dots to mainly via electrostatic attraction and hydrophobic interaction. More acidic environment leads to the less dissociation of the acidic surface functional groups on C-dots and thus less negatively charged C-dots, substantially weakening their electrostatic interaction with DOX. In addition, the amino moiety of DOX can be protonated more easily at lower pH, resulting in the improved water solubility and thus weakening its hydrophobic interaction with C-dots. Therefore, acidic environment can accelerate the release of DOX from C-dots, facilitating tumor targeting and the decrease of drug side effects [179].

In addition, drug-controlled release can also be achieved using NIR radiation with any carrier that can convert NIR emission to local heat. The elevated temperature induced by local heat can weaken the interactions between the carrier and the drug, resulting in the increase of the drug mobility [163]. For example, a C-dot-based DDS exhibiting pH/NIR light dual-responsive drug delivery property is synthesized by using PEG macromonomers complexed with chitosan and C-dots as the precursor. In this design, the pH-sensitive chitosan dominates pH-regulated drug release due to its pH-sensitive swelling/deswelling over the range of 5.0–7.4, while the thermosensitive nonlinear PEG network dominates NIR light-regulated drug release due to the local heat produced by the embedded C-dots [180]. NIR-triggered drug release is very promising, because it offers the possibility of rapid drug release when necessary.

Photosensitizer chlorine e6 (Ce6) has also been successfully conjugated onto the C-dot surface for simultaneous biological imaging and photodynamic therapy (PDT) [181]. The C-dots with amino-terminated surface were prepared using PEG2000N as the surface passivation agent, and then, Ce6 was covalently conjugated onto the surface of C-dots via carbodiimide chemistry. The C-dots-Ce6 has been demonstrated for improved photosensitizer fluorescence detection (PFD) and PDT by two different excitation pathways: indirect excitation by FRET from the C-dots to Ce6 and direct excitation of the Ce6. The C-dots-Ce6 exhibits excellent capability of bioimaging and tumor homing, thus enabling NIR fluorescence imaging-guided PDT treatment.

Theranostics, the combination of diagnostics and therapy for potential personalized medicine, represents one of the most exciting research fields in nanomedicine, although the current distinction between DDS and theranostics is blurred [163].

Due to their excellent biocompatibility and photostability, C-dot-based theranostic systems have been investigated extensively. For example, through the covalent conjugation of anticancer drug-oxidized oxaliplatin (Oxa) onto the C-dot surface, C-dots-Oxa successfully integrates the C-dot imaging property and the therapy performance of Oxa. Tracking the distribution of Oxa in H22 liver-cancer-bearing mouse has been realized by monitoring the fluorescence signal of C-dots-Oxa. This might help customize the injection time and dosage of the drug and minimize side effects of anticancer drugs [182]. Similarly, cisplatin (IV) prodrug has also been covalently conjugated onto C-dot surface to develop a theranostic system, C-dots-Pt(IV), which exhibits enhanced tumor-inhibition efficacy while showing negligible side effects. Fig. 5.22 A illustrates the drug delivery process of C-dots-Pt(IV): Firstly, blood circulation of C-dots-Pt(IV) is prolonged owing to negative charge/PEGylation, and then, the EPR effect results in its accumulation at the tumor site. After its responsiveness to acidic tumor microenvironment, C-dots-Pt(IV) is effectively uptaken by cancer cells. With the assistance of “proton sponge” effect, endosome escape occurs, and in the meantime, cisplatin is released from cisplatin(IV) prodrug in a controllable manner. At last, the released cisplatin binds with DNA to induce apoptosis of cancer cells [183].

In the examples mentioned above, the conjugation of different functional moieties onto C-dot surface is often required, which always increase the difficulties in their preparation and decrease the controllability and drug-loading efficiency. Therefore, the development of intrinsic C-dots without any surface functionalization but exhibiting the properties of the simultaneous precise diagnosis and effective drug delivery is greatly desired.

Wang and coworkers prepared red-emissive C-dots that exhibit an emission peak at 640nm. Under 671nm irradiation, the red-emissive C-dots also exhibit photothermal conversion efficiency as high as 38.5%, due to their strong absorption in NIR region. These intrinsic properties of the C-dots make the FL and PA imaging possible and allow simultaneous photothermal therapy (PTT) without any extra surface functionalization. PA and FL imaging-guided PTT caused substantial empyrosis at the tumor site, thus remarkably suppressing tumor growth [157]. The same research group also prepared a C-dot-based intrinsic theranostic system for imaging-guided cancer therapy combing PDT and PTT. The as-prepared C-dots exhibited 27% 1O2 generating efficiency and 36.2% photothermal conversion efficiency under NIR laser irradiation (635nm) [185].

Due to the poor water solubility and low photostability and bioavailability, the applications of organic NIR chromophores are greatly limited, although there are bright prospects for the simultaneous imaging diagnosis and cancer treatment with PTT. Zheng and coworkers synthesized a C-dot-based intrinsic theranostic system by solvothermal treatment of cyanine dye and PEG800. Due to their favorable NIR emission and 38.7% photothermal conversion efficiency, simultaneous NIR imaging and PTT treatment in vitro and in vivo are successfully achieved by using the as-prepared C-dots. As confirmed in Fig. 5.22B, C-dots are accumulated in the tumor site, and the fluorescence signal is peaked at 6h and plateau within 6–12h. The in vivo experiments revealed that the tumors were effectively suppressed with the tumor inhibitory rate as high as 91% upon PTT treatment (Fig. 5.22C) [184].

Because of their successful applications in chemo-/biosensing, bioimaging, DDS, and theranostics, C-dots quickly become an important tool in nanobiotechnology and nanomedicine. However, significant improvements on C-dots are still required; especially, there is still a lack of red- and NIR-emissive C-dots. Despite C-dot emission spectrum that redshifts with increasing excitation wavelength, the emission intensity also decreases. In particular, the weak emission of C-dots in the NIR region restricts the biomedical applications of C-dots. PLQY is another essential parameter that should be continuously optimized, in order to maximize C-dot fluorescence signal while minimizing their cytotoxicity. Therefore, the preparation of red- and NIR-emissive C-dots with high PLQY in a facile and efficient way still requires in-depth exploration. Once the problems about the practical red-emissive C-dots are solved, a multiplexed detection systems based on different fluorescent color C-dots can be realized.

In regard to the multimodal imaging, now, the dual-modal imaging probes based on C-dots are continually reported by many research groups. However, in order to further improve the reliability of the diagnosis, more signal overlay could bring a more trustable result. Considering about this, more imaging modalities should be incorporated that will bring out probes with more modalities gathering. Furthermore, with the increasing demands for nanomedicine, scientists are now pursuing the aim that to assemble diagnosis and therapy onto one single nanomaterial. In this way, diagnosis and therapy can achieve simultaneously, which can save the time of illness treatments and increase the cure rate. Therefore, the theranostic applications of C-dots should also aim at these directions, though the rational design and development of theranostic systems using C-dots are still very challenging.

In summary, the fascinating characteristics and perspectives of C-dots will continue to attract substantial research attentions from different fields. Stimulated by the challenges of C-dots, more research is desired to better the C-dots for their biomedical applications as long as we would like to make full use of them.
